coredata.dc:title: Morphology formation mechanism and electrochemical performance of poly(o-phenylenediamine) based electrode materials
coredata.dc:creator: Lan, Haidie
Muslim, Arzugul
Hojiahmat, Mehriban
Wang, Lin
Meng, Yu
Zhong, Qing
coredata.dc:description: Polystyrene-b-poly(acrylic acid) (PS57-b-PAA54) is prepared by the Atom Transfer Radical Polymerization (ATRP), and poly(o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD) and its carbon dots composites (CDs/PoPD) are synthesized by using the self-assembled micelles of PS57-b-PAA54 as template. Rod-shaped PoPD and CDs/PoPD particles are formed with the average diameters of 263 nm and 322 nm, respectively. The PoPD exhibit a wide size distribution and diverse morphologies, while the CDs/PoPD is mostly composed of olive-shaped particles with a narrow size distribution. The PS57-b-PAA54 induced morphological formation mechanism of PoPD and CDs/PoPD is then proposed. The electrochemical performances test characterized by cyclic voltammetry, chronopotential, AC impedance and constant current charge-discharge tests in a three-electrode system reveals that the initial specific discharge capacitances of CDs/PoPD and PoPD can reach up to 1316 F·g−1 and 216 F·g−1, respectively, at the current density of 1 A·g−1. CDs/PoPD shows better cycle stability with the specific discharge capacitance of 830 F·g−1 for the first cycle, and the decline to 357 F·g−1 in 2000 cycles at the current density of 1 A·g−1 within the potential window of −0.2–1.4 V.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Block copolymer template
Polystyrene-b-poly(acrylic acid)
Poly(o-phenylenediamine)
Carbon dots
Morphology formation mechanism
Capacitance characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Polymerizations of oPD and CDs/oPD with PS57-b-PAA54 template and template removal
Structural characterization of CDs, PoPD and CDs/PoPD
Morphological characterization of PoPD and CDs/PoPD
Electrochemical properties of PoPD and CDs/PoPD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra (a), X-ray diffraction patterns (b) and UV–vis absorption spectra (c) of CDs, PoPD and CDs/PoPD with template removed.
TEM images of PoPD (a) and CDs/PoPD (b) obtained at operation voltage of 200 kV and that of CDs/PoPD obtained at the operation voltage of 80 kV (c); (d) shows the HRTEM image of CDs/PoPD.

Particle size distributions of PS57-b-PAA54 micelles (a), PoPD (b) and CDs/PoPD (c) obtained from TEM images, and the particle size distributions of PoPD (d) and CDs/PoPD (e) obtained with Malvern particle size analyzer.
Cyclic voltammetry curves of PoPD (a) and CDs/PoPD (b).
Chronopotentiograms of PoPD (a) and CDs/PoPD (b), and their discharge specific capacitance curves at different current densities (c) and AC impedance spectra (d).
Cyclic discharge of CDs/PoPD at the constant current of 1 A·g−1.
PS-b-PAA-induced morphological formation mechanism of PoPD and CDs/PoPD.
Synthesis route to block copolymer PS57-b- PAA54.

head.ce:title: Morphology formation mechanism and electrochemical performance of poly(o-phenylenediamine) based electrode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Energy Storage and Photoelectrocatalytic Materials, Urumqi 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Energy Storage and Photoelectrocatalytic Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Energy Storage and Photoelectrocatalytic Materials, Urumqi 830054, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemical Engineering Center, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electrochemical Engineering Center, Xinjiang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemical Engineering Center, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Haidie Lan and Arzugul Muslim contribute to the work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polystyrene-b-poly(acrylic acid) (PS57-b-PAA54) is prepared by the Atom Transfer Radical Polymerization (ATRP), and poly(o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD) and its carbon dots composites (CDs/PoPD) are synthesized by using the self-assembled micelles of PS57-b-PAA54 as template. Rod-shaped PoPD and CDs/PoPD particles are formed with the average diameters of 263 nm and 322 nm, respectively. The PoPD exhibit a wide size distribution and diverse morphologies, while the CDs/PoPD is mostly composed of olive-shaped particles with a narrow size distribution. The PS57-b-PAA54 induced morphological formation mechanism of PoPD and CDs/PoPD is then proposed. The electrochemical performances test characterized by cyclic voltammetry, chronopotential, AC impedance and constant current charge-discharge tests in a three-electrode system reveals that the initial specific discharge capacitances of CDs/PoPD and PoPD can reach up to 1316 F·g−1 and 216 F·g−1, respectively, at the current density of 1 A·g−1. CDs/PoPD shows better cycle stability with the specific discharge capacitance of 830 F·g−1 for the first cycle, and the decline to 357 F·g−1 in 2000 cycles at the current density of 1 A·g−1 within the potential window of −0.2–1.4 V.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing shortage of sustainable energy, the interests in and demands on energy storage materials and devices have been greatly increased [1], especially for those with high power densities, high charge-discharge rates, and good cycle stability. Therefore, a varity of supercapacitors have been developed attentions as a miniature energy storage system and widely used in a variety of fields, such as hybrid vehicles, power backup systems, industrial power, and so on [2–4]. Based on their energy storage principles, electrochemical capacitors can be divided into electric double-layer capacitors and Faraday pseudocapacitors [5]. In recent years, transition metal oxides and conductive polymers have been mainly used as the electrode materials for pseudocapacitors [6,7]. The conductive polymers are used in the supercapacitor electrodes and components to bridge the gap between the existing carbon-based supercapacitors and batteries to form units of intermediate specific energy [8]. Conductive polymer-based electrode materials represented by polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (PPY), poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) and their derivatives have shown many advantages, such as low-cost, easy synthesis, high specific capacitances, and high energy densities. Their π-electron conjugate systems provide favorable conditions for the electron transport in the redox reaction [8,9]. PANI is considered to be the most promising conductive polymer, and the continuous exploration of its electrochemical performance has driven research interest in PANI derivatives in various fields. PANI [10–12] and its composites with carbon-based nanomaterials [12–17] and metal oxides [18–20] have been widely studied as electrode materials in supercapacitors with high specific discharge capacity, but their cycle stabilities and energy densities are still poor.

Aromatic diamine polymers also have attracted much attentions because of their highly active free amino groups and imine groups that can be further modified [21]. Among them, poly(o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD) shows the advantages of novel structure, variable conductivity, strong electrical activity, optomagnetic activity, good environmental stability, and thermal stability, which provides the possibility of expanding the application of conductive polymers [22]. Through the electrocatalysis on reduced graphene oxide, Yadegari et al. [23] prepared a composite of graphene oxide and PoPD, whose discharge specific capacitance at 0.1 A·g−1 reached to 308.5 F·g−1. At 1 A·g−1, its capacity retention rate after 1500 cycles was 99%. Yang et al. [24] prepared the similar composite through in-situ growth of PoPD on the reduction site of graphene in order to creat high-performance supercapacitors. At 1 A·g−1, the discharge specific capacitance in 1 mol/L H2SO4 reached to 381 F·g−1. Sačer et al. [25] used PoPD to functionally modify graphene electrodes with chemical and electrochemical methods, respectively, and obtained composite materials with better capacitance characteristics within a larger potential window than pure PoPD. Pisarevskaya et al. [26] prepared a new ternary electroactive composite including silicotungstic acid, PoPD and reduced graphene with electrochemical methods. Study showed that this material could be used in portable electrochemical sensors. Other scientists also studied the applications of N, O-doped carbon-based materials, PoPD composites [27,28] or PoPD-containing copolymers [29] in supercapacitors. However, these studies are mainly based on PoPD-based composites, there are few reports on the capacitance characteristics of PoPD itself. Meanwhile, the performance of the PoPD-based electrode materials reported in the literature obviously can still be improved.

Nanostructured conductive polymers have become the main candidate materials for active electrodes of energy storage devices due to their advantages of large ion or electron transport interfaces and short charge transport distances [30]. Their structures can be tuned by many methods, such as template method with block copolymers, the one of the most promising methods. However, the poor mechanical properties of conductive polymers can significantly affect the capacitance performance of electrode during the frequent swelling and shrinking for the charge and discharge, and thus it is difficult to achieve the fast electron transfer for the Faraday process [31,32].

In the present work, o-phenylenediamine (oPD) was composited with carbon dots (CDs) using a block copolymer, PS-b-PAA, as the template to improve the electrochemical performance of its polymer, while making up for the limited specific capacitance of the microstructure of carbon-containing materials [33,34]. In our previous study, PEO-b-PAA with two blocks of similar thermodynamic compatibilities was used as the template which made the self-assembly condition control of micelle very stringent and the tuning of the polymer morphology very cumbersome [35]. The amphiphilic block copolymer PS-b-PAA is consisted of PS and PAA blocks with significantly different thermodynamic properties and can self-assemble to form vesicle micelles. Both oPD and CDs-oPD can form hydrogen bonds with the -COOH in the block polymer via the -NH2 in the monomer or the -OH on the surface of CDs, thereby being trapped in the block copolymer template. Fewer oPD or CDs-oPD particles adsorbed onto the internal surface of the template vesicle than onto the external surface, causing uneven stresses that can damage the vesicle wall. The different steric hindrances and numbers of active sites of oPD and CDs-oPD cause different numbers of particles per unit area adsorbed on the two sides of the vesicle wall, resulting in the conductive polymer particles with different sizes and morphologies. Scheme 1 shows the formation mechanism of the particle morphologies induced by the block copolymer. As for the morphology of conducting polymers plays an important role in their electrochemical performance [36], the electrochemical tests on PoPD and CDs/PoPD have been done.

Styrene (St, Macklin) distilled under vacuum after being dehydrated on CaH2. tert-B utyl acrylate (tBA, Macklin) was puriﬁed through vacuum distillation. o-phenylenediamine (oPD, Tianjin Chemical Co., Ltd.), CuBr was dried under vacuum after being washed with acetic acid and ethanol. Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and Dichloromethane were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Reagent Plant. N,N,N′,N”,N ”-pentamethyl diethylene triamine (PMDETA, Aldrich). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Ltd.) was dried by anhydrous magnesium sulfate and distilled under reduced pressure. Tetrahydrofuran (THF, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute). N′N-ammonium persulfate (APS, Acros). Methanol and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd.

CDs and PS57-b-PAA54 (with
M
n
̅
calculated from H NMR of 12840 g·mol−1 and PDI of 1.04) were prepared as described in our previous work [37]. The detailed processes can be found in the Supplementary Material (Figs. S1−S4). The two-block copolymer PS57-b-PtBA54 was first synthesized by the ATRP method and then hydrolyzed to afford the pH-responsive amphiphilic block copolymer PS57-b-PAA54 ( Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymerizations of oPD and CDs/oPD with PS57-b-PAA54 template and template removal
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of oPD added into 10 mL PS57-b- PAA54 solution (10 mL of distilled water was added dropwised into the solution composed of 1 mg of PS57-b-PAA54 dissolved in 1 mL of THF) and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 3 h. The initiator, ammonium persulfate (APS) in 1 mL of distilled water was then added dropwise into the above solution at the monomer to initiator mass ratio of 2:1. The reaction solutions were further stirred for 3 h, suction filtered, and vacuum-dried at room temperature for 24 h to afford the composite of template and PoPD. A small amount composite was sampled for the related tests.
A certain amount of CDs (6% of total mass) was ground with oPD monomer to obtain CDs-oPD. Then the composite of template and CDs/PoPD was prepared by the same procedure as above.

The PS57-b- PAA54 template of the remaining samples was removed by sequentially ultrasonicating the sample in methanol, ethanol, and tetrahydrofuran (THF) until the solution became colorless to afford PoPD and CDs/PoPD particles.

IR spectra were measured with a BRUKER-TNSOR-27 FTIR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellet. Diffraction patterns were recorded on a Bruker D2 Advance X-ray diffraction spectrometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source at the operation voltage of 40 kV and operation current of 250 mA. Uv-vis absorption spectra in the range of 300–800 cm−1 were obtained with a HI-TACHI U-3310 Uv-vis spectrometer. TEM and HRTEM images were captured with a JEOL JEM-1200 EX/S scanning electron microscope at the acceleration voltage of 200 kV, or a JEOL JEM-1230 HC scanning electron microscope at the acceleration voltage of 80 kV. The particle size was measured with a Zetasizer Nano ZS 90 Malvern nano particle size analyzer. PANI was ultrasonically dispersed with absolute ethanol for the test.

The electrodes coated with PoPD and CDs/PoPD were prepared and their electrochemical properties were characterized in a three-electrode system. Briefly, PoPD or CDs/PoPD, carbon black, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTEF) were mixed at the mass ratio of 7:1:1, spread on a graphite electrode, and vacuum dried at room temperature for 24 h to obtain the working electrode for the three-electrode system. A platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode and an Ag-AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted on a CHI-660 C electrochemical workstation with 0.3 mol·L−1 perchloric acid (HClO4) as the electrolyte. The potential window for the cyclic voltammetry was set to −0.2–1.4 v, and the AC impedance was measured under the open circuit voltage at the sweep frequency from 0.01 Hz to 1 MHz, and the amplitude of 5 mV. The galvanostatic charge-discharge curves (GCD) were measured at the current density of 1 A·g−1 in a Land CT 2001A battery test system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CDs, PoPD and CDs/PoPD
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of PoPD and CDs/PoPD before and after the template removed suggest that both of them have been successfully prepared using the PS57-b- PAA54 template and the template has been removed (Fig. S5). Fig. 1a shows the IR spectra of CDs, and PoPD and CDs/PoPD with the template removed. CDs exhibits absorption peaks at 3410 cm−1, 1780 cm−1, 1621 cm−1, 1360 cm−1, 1240 cm−1, 1159 cm−1, 1041 cm−1, and 840 cm−1 which can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C˭O in the surface carboxylic function group, the stretching vibration of C˭C, the vibration of residual water, the stretching vibration of C‒O in the carboxyl group, and the out-of-plane bending vibration of C‒H, respectively [12]. PoPD shows the characteristic absorption peaks of the stretching vibrations of N‒H and O‒H in the amine group at 3430–3400 cm−1 [21,38]. The peaks at 1530 cm−1, 1378 cm−1 and 1238 cm−1 can be respectively ascribed to the vibration of benzene ring, and the stretching vibrations of the C‒N bond in benzene ring and quinone ring [39]. The out-of-plane bending vibration of the benzene ring C‒H bond in phenazine skeleton causes the absorption peaks at 843 cm−1, 755 cm−1 and 617 cm−1. The peaks at ~754 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of the C‒N bond between the benzene ring and the amine group and the rocking vibration of N‒H in amine group [40]. The stretching vibrations of the C˭C in the benzene and quinone of both PoPD and CDs/PoPD show the absorption peaks at ~1520 cm−1, and the peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C˭N [22]. The composition with CDs slightly red shifts the absorption peaks of PoPD, suggesting that the degree of conjugation is increased [32,41]. Multiple characteristic absorption peaks of the functional groups on the surface of CDs appear in the CDs/PoPD, indicating that the composite of CDs and PoPD has been successfully prepared.

CDs, and PoPD and CDs/ PoPD with template removed were further characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1b, CDs exhibits a strong peak at 2θ = 26.7°, and several weak diffraction peaks (Fig. 1b). The strong diffraction peaks of PoPD at 2θ = 7.3°, 11.7°, 12.0°, 15.7°, 17.9°, 21.6° and 25.2° are symmetrical and sharp, suggesting that the degree of crystallinity of PoPD surface is high. PoPD also exhibits some weak diffraction peaks, consistent with those of the PoPD prepared by chemical oxidation polymerization [42]. All diffraction peaks of CDs are found in CDs/PoPD, but the peak at 26.7° becomes slightly weaker and broader. The diffraction peaks of PoPD in the composite are either weaker or red-shifted. These results indicate that the composition changes the microstructure of PoPD to a certain extent, but shows no significant effects on the crystal structure of CDs. The composite exhibit the structural feature with CDs coated on the surface of PoPD, and some PoPD are embedded in the spatial framework of CDs [43].

PoPD and CDs/ PoPD with the template removed were respectively dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), sonicated, kept still and measured for UV–vis absorption spectra in the range of 200–800 nm. As shown in Fig. 1c, both PoPD and CDs/ PoPD exhibit strong absorption peaks at 275 nm due to the π-π* transition on the benzene ring [39]. The π-π* transition between the benzene and quinone rings and the n-π* transition in the phenolic unit lead to the absorption peaks at 375 nm and 425 nm, respectively [21,38,44,45]. The wavelength of maximum absorption red shifts in the CDs/ PoPD, indicating that the increase of carbon content promotes the prolongation of the conjugated chain of polymer. The absorption peak for the π-π* transition on benzene ring at 275 nm is slightly blue-shifted, possibly due to the opposite effect caused by CDs doping that hinders the excitation of polarons [46]. These results further indicate that CDs and PoPD have been successfully composited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of PoPD and CDs/PoPD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of PoPD and CDs/ PoPD obtained under different conditions. The vesicular template micelles were prepared by dropwise adding 10 mL of distilled water into 1 mg/mL of PS57-b-PAA54 solution in THF under constant stirring (Fig. S3). The average diameter of the micelles is measured to be 372 nm with the standard deviation of 115.05, suggesting that its size distribution is broad ( Fig. 3a). The -COOH group on PAA block in the template micelle can form hydrogen bonds with the amino group and imino group of the monomer, and then hydrogen bond can be formed between the molecules. Therefore, the micelles can act as a morphology-guiding agent to induce the polymer morphology and size from the monomer with the initiator. The morphology of PoPD induced by the micelles are rod-like shapes with the average diameter of 263.0 nm and the standard deviation of 123.53 (Fig. 3b). It has been reported that hydrogen bonding and decomplexation can cause reversible transitions between vesicles or hollow spheres and core-shell micelles [47,48]. The PS block can function as the vesicle wall and the PAA block swells on the inner and outer surfaces of vesicle wall. During the polymerization of PoPD, oPD forms hydrogen bonds with template micelles, causing the collapse and shrink of the block. The different numbers of oPD attached to the inner and outer of vesicle surface break the morphology of the vesicle. The hollow vesicle is expanded into a sheet and eventually "torn" in the particles of different sizes. Due to the small number of oPD on the inner vesicle wall, and the small sizes and ununiform stacking density on the outer wall, the torn sheet-like aggregates show significantly different morphologies and sizes. Therefore, PoPD particles formed by the aggregates induced polymerization show the different particle sizes after the template removed with the PDI of 0.92, as demonstrated by the Malvern particle size analysis. The average particle size of PoPD obtained with the Malvern particle size analyzer is 457 nm, significantly larger than that obtained from the TEM images. It can be explained that the particle size is measured in solution where the particles may swell. In addition, Malvern particle size analyzer is not used for the measurement of average diameter.

The polymerization of CDs-oPD in the template forms spherical and olive particles with the average diameter of 321.6 nm and the standard deviation of 73.34 (Fig. 2b). The smaller standard deviation of particle diameter suggests the uniform morphology and size of CDs/PoPD, which is also confirmed in the TEM imaging of CDs/PoPD d at lower acceleration voltage (Fig. 2c). The average particle size of CDs/ PoPD is determined to be 395 nm (Fig. 3e) and the PDI is found to be 0.57, which is much smaller than those of PoPD. The particle size measured with the Malvern particle size analyzer is also larger than the TEM result, but the difference is not as large as that for PoPD. It can be explained that, unlike PoPD particles, the CDs/PoPD particles possess similar length-to-diameter ratios, and thus the overall particle size is close to that obtained by TEM.

The obvious contrast in the TEM image of CDs/PoPD suggests that the PoPD surface is covered with CDs (Fig. 2c), consistent with the XRD results. Fig. 2d shows the HRTEM image of CDs/PoPD, where the dark area represents CDs, and the gray area corresponds to PoPD. XRD analysis reveals that neither of them possesses a complete crystal structure, and thus no obvious lattice fringes are observed in the HRTEM image. Despite its more uniform morphology and size, the average particle size of CDs/PoPD is larger than that of PoPD. It can be explained that the composition of oPD with CDs results in higher steric hindrance, and thus the enter of CDs-oPD into the vesicle is subject to higher resistance. The difference in the numbers of CDs-oPD on the inner and outer wall surface of the vesicle is more significant, and thus there are fewer “tearing” sites and the vesicle is torn into larger aggregates. The more uniform sizes of CDs/PoPD can be explained that, despite the fewer CDs-oPD on the inner surface of vesicle wall, unlike oPD, it does not form a dense and uneven accumulation on the outer surface of the vesicle wall. In addition, fewer CDs-oPD attached to per unit area of vesicle wall than oPD. The stress difference between the per unit area inner and outer surfaces of vesicle wall is more uniform than that caused by oPD, which inevitably leads to the relatively uniform sizes and morphologies of the torn template aggregates. This explanation is also supported by the narrower particle size distribution of CDs/PoPD (Fig. 3c and e). Based on these results, it can be concluded that the PS57-b-PAA54 micelles induce the polymerization of oPD and CDs-oPD by hydrogen bonding, and their different steric hindrances cause the different morphologies of PoPD and CDs/PoPD particles. Scheme 1 shows their morphological formation mechanism.

It is worth noting that the size distributions of PANI and CDs/PANI prepared with the same template are exactly the opposite to the size distribution characteristics of PoPD and CDs/PoPD, e.g. the PDI of PANI is smaller [34]. The possible reason is that oPD contains two -NH2 groups, while ANI has only one. Therefore, the former has twice number of active sites of the latter for the adsorption onto the vesicle wall. The amount of ANI adsorbed on the outer surface of vesicle wall is much smaller than that of oPD, but the numbers of ANI and oPD that can enter the vesicle and attach to the inner surface of vesicle wall are limited, and possible similar. The larger number of oPD on the outer surface of vesicle wall causes the broader size distribution of PoPD particles. Although the steric hindrance CDs/PANI is higher than PANI, the functional groups on the surfaces of CDs can make up for the "deletion" of interaction site between PANI and template shell, the PAA block, to a certain extent to ensure enough CDs-ANI attached onto the outer surface of vesicle wall. In addition, different functional groups, such as ‒NH2 and ‒OH, form hydrogen bonds with the ‒COOH in PAA differently, causing a "chaotic" stacking of CDs-ANI on the vesicle wall surface. The vesicle wall are then “torn” into aggregates of different sizes and morphologies, by which the CDs/PANI induced shows a broader size distribution than that of PANI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of PoPD and CDs/PoPD
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical properties of PoPD and CDs/PoPD were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronopotential, and AC impedance in a three-electrode system. Fig. 4 shows the CV curves of PoPD and CDs/PoPD obtained at different sweep rates using 0.3 mol·L−1 perchloric acid as the electrolyte. Neither PoPD nor CDs/PoPD exhibits obvious redox peaks, indicating that they have certain electrochemical activities and the electrochemical process involves pseudocapacitance [21]. The integral areas of their CV curves are different, suggesting that their capacitance activities are different. In the potential window of − 0.2–1.4 V and in the sweep rate range of 10–100 mV·s−1, the integral area increases, and the peak potential also gradually increases with the increase of sweep rate, indicating that the capacitance declines with sweep rate. It can be explained that frequent swelling and shrinking destruct the ion migration channels in the polymer. The polymer structure collapses, and consequently the ion absorption/desorption or the surface active substances are reduced during the long charge/ discharge cycles [49]. It should be noted that the electrode materials show better electrochemical performance in a wide potential window. Chi et al. [50] pointed out that the reasonable electrode design could suppress the discharge of water, and the upper limit voltage of Ag/AgCl could be increased to as high as 1.5 V. Additionally, the overoxidation of polymer at a relatively high potential was closely related to the counter-ion characteristics provided by the electrolyte solution. Tang et al. [51] reported that, when the counterion was ClO4−, it was conducive to the increase of the upper voltage limit because it can make the dipole avoided from the nucleophilic attack of OH- and H2O better. Furthermore, because the electrode material had good electrical conductivity, the hydrogen storage effect of carbon material could be increased the energy density or inhibit water discharge due to the reduction of internal resistance, thus to expand its voltage window [52,53]. In addition, the shape of CV curve changes, that is, with the increase of scanning speed, the peak current value (i p) was increased and the peak potential difference (ΔE p) was slightly increased, indicating the existence of ohmic potential drop [54], which proved the contribution of Ohm’s law [54]. Therefore, we calculated the specific discharge capacity at different scanning speeds according to Eq. (1) [55], and the results were listed in Table S1.
(1)
C =
1
m (
V
2
−
V
1
)
∫
V
1
V
2
(
Q V
)
d V =
1
m (
V
2
−
V
1
)
∫
V
1
V
2
(
i s
)
d V
where, m represents the mass of the active substance, V1 and V2 represent low and high potentials respectively, s represents scanning rate, and I represents current. Compared with those of PoPD, the peaks of CDs/PoPD are more symmetrical and more rectangular with larger peak areas, combined with the calculation results in Table S1, it can be seen that the capacitance of the composite is better than PoPD alone. UV–vis absorption analysis indicates that the conjugated chain of CDs/PoPD is longer. The long conjugated structure provides more channels for electron transfer, and thus can improve the electrochemical properties of the polymer.

The chronopotentiograms of PoPD and CDs/PoPD were measured in the three-electrode system with the carbon electrode coated with PoPD or CDs/PoPD as the working electrode, a platinum electrode as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode at different current densities. The chronopotential curves in the current density range from 1 A·g−1 to 3 3 A·g−1 are basically symmetrical, indicating their good electrochemical reversibility ( Fig. 5a and b). The specific discharge capacitance can be obtained with Eq. (2).
(2)
SC = I Δ t / Δ E · m
where SC (F·g−1) is the specific discharge capacitance, I (A) is the discharge current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, ΔE (V) is the voltage change during the discharge, and m(g) is the mass of the active material. The specific discharge capacitances at current densities in the range of 1–3 A·g−1 are then calculated and plotted (Table S2 and Fig. 5c). The specific discharge capacitance of PoPD and CDs/PoPD are calculated to be 216 F·g−1 and 1316 F·g−1, respectively at the current density of 1 A·g−1, and gradually decease with the increase of current density, consistent with the CV results. The specific capacitances of PoPD and CDs/PoPD decrease to 14 F·g−1 and 137 F·g−1 as the current density increased to 3 A·g−1, indicating that their rate performances are not ideal, and the capacitance property of CDs/PoPD is better.

For comparison purpose, the hydrolysis time of PtBA and the self-assembly conditions of PS57-b-PAA54 are set the same as those for the preparation of PoPD using PEO45-b-PAA35 template in our previous work [35]. The specific discharge capacitance of the PoPD prepared with PEO45-b-PAA35 template is found to be 660 F·g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1, much higher than the PoPD prepared in the present work. It can be explained with the larger particle size of the PoPD prepared using the block copolymer micelles with long core-shell sequence chains as the template, and the relatively small surface area of the active material where ions migrate in and out. In addition, the alcoholic hydroxyl group in the front part of PEO in PEO45-b-PAA35 can also attract more active substances to the template micelles via hydrogen bonding, which may make the final product more electrochemically active. However, the self-assembly conditions of the template used in the present work are simpler than those of the PEO45-b-PAA35 template used out previous work. The micellization of PEO-b-PAA requires the partial hydrolysis and certain hydrophobicity of the PAA block, and is strongly dependent on the acidity and alkalinity of the medium. In contrast, the micellization of PS-b-PAA is not affect by these factors. Therefore, if the hydrolysis time of PS57-b-PAA54 is prolonged, enhancing the hydrophilicity of PAA is beneficial to the formation of more uniformly dispersed micelles as a dopant [56], and thus improves the electrochemical activity of prepared polymer. The specific discharge capacitance of CDs/PoPD is much higher than that of PoPD prepared in our previous work, due to the synergistic effect of CDs and PoPD.

The interfacial electron transfer properties and impedance changes of the prepared polymers were then investigated by AC impedance. As shown in Fig. 5d, both AC impedance spectra of PoPD and CDs/PoPD consist of a semicircle in the high frequency region and a straight line in the low frequency region. The diameter of the semicircle reflects the charge transfer resistance. It is clear that the charge transfer resistance at the interface between CDs/PoPD electrode and electrolyte is lower than that at the interface between PoPD electrode and electrolyte. The greater the slope of the straight line in the low frequency region, the stronger the pseudocapacitance of the material. The greater slope of CDs/PoPD suggests its pseudocapacitance performance is better. In all, the AC impedance analysis suggests that the internal charge transfer resistance of CDs/PoPD is lower and its electrochemical performance is better, consistent with the CV and chronopotential results.

Since CDs/PoPD exhibited ideal chronopotentiometric properties, its cyclic charge/discharge performance was then evaluated at the current density of 1 A·g−1. As shown in Fig. 6, the specific discharge capacitance is 830 F·g−1 at the first cycle, and declines to 357 F·g−1, with the total attenuation of 56.99%, in 2000 cycles. It is worth noting that the specific capacitance declines 38.34% in the first 50 cycles, and then remains almost constant thereafter, suggesting better cycle stability of CDs/PoPD in the later stage. Conductive polymers are prone to swell and shrink during charging and discharging [31,32], which causes the rapid stability declines at the beginning of the test. However, the specific discharge capacitance of CDs/PoPD is maintained at a high level in 2000 charge and discharge cycles. It can be explained that the repeated operation connects the blind hole and mesopores in the deep parts of the electrode active material, and thus the active surface area is increased [57]. In addition, the composition with CDs improve the conductivity or redox behaviors of the conductive polymer [36] and inhibit volume swelling/shrinkage during the continuous charging and discharging process [58]. The cycle stability of CDs/PoPD is better than that of the PoPD prepared in our previous work [35] at 0.5 A·g−1 (75.04% attenuation in 1800 cycles), indicating that the incorporation of CDs is the main contributor to good electrochemical properties of the composite.

In summary, PS57-b-PAA54 was successfully synthesized by the ATRP method and its self-assembled micelles were used as the template to prepare PoPD and CDs/PoPD micro/nanoparticles. The particle sizes and size distributions characterized by TEM and Malvern size analyzer reveal that the morphologies and sizes of PoPD and CDs/PoPD induced by PS57-b-PAA54 template are determined by the difference between the stresses on inner and outer surfaces of the vesicle-like template micelle wall. The different steric hindrances and numbers of active sites of oPD and CDs-oPD result in different stacking densities on the two sides of the vesicle wall, which causes the different stresses. The particle morphology and size, as well as the composition with the carbon-based materials, CDs, contribute to the good electrochemical performance of CDs/PoPD significantly. Therefore, more comprehensive tests were conducted for the capacitance properties of PoPD and CDs/PoPD. The chronopotential analysis reveals that the initial specific discharge capacitances of CDs/PoPD and PoPD are measured by chronopotential analysis to 1316 F·g−1 and 216 F·g−1, respectively, at the current density of 1 A·g−1. CDs/PoPD exhibits better cycle stability with the capacitance declined from 830 F·g−1 of the first cycle to 357 F·g−1 at the 2000th cycle.

For comparison purpose, the hydrolysis time and self-assembly conditions for PEO-PAA reported in the previous work are adopted for the self-assembly of PS-PAA. Therefore, the work may not be enough to support our hypothesis that the PS and PAA with significantly different thermodynamic properties would overcome the harsh conditions required for the micellization of PEO-PAA. Further work is undergoing in our lab.
The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.
We would like to thank National Natural Science Foundation of China for the financial support (No. 51763023).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116688.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
